patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH v3 2/3] btrfs: fix incorrect buffered IO fallback for append direct writes

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v7 1/3] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

version_history.subject: [PATCH v7 1/3] tracing/lock: Remove unnecessary linux/sched.h include

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes an unnecessary include statement in the lock tracing header file, as none of the trace events reference anything from linux/sched.h.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v7 3/3] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

version_history.subject: [PATCH v7 3/3] locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to sleepable locks

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new tracepoint called contended_release to the Linux kernel's locking mechanism. It fires when a lock is released under contention, allowing for correlation of lock hold time with waiter events by lock address. The patch modifies the slowpath unlock paths of various sleepable locks, including mutexes, rtmutexes, semaphores, and rwsems, to enable this tracepoint. The placement of the tracepoint varies depending on the lock type, but it generally fires before the lock is released or after the release but before the wake. This patch also adds support for reader/writer locks, firing the tracepoint for every reader releasing while a writer is waiting.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v7 2/3] locking/percpu-rwsem: Extract __percpu_up_read()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch extracts the slowpath of percpu_up_read() into a new function __percpu_up_read(). The original function is simplified, and the slowpath is moved to a separate function to avoid increasing binary size due to the addition of a tracepoint. The new function is exported as a symbol, allowing it to be used elsewhere in the kernel.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v7 0/3] locking: contended_release tracepoint instrumentation
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a new tracepoint, contended_release, which fires on the lock holder's side when a lock is released while there are waiters. This provides hold time estimation and allows for capturing the holder's stack at release time.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Acked the patch series without any objections or suggestions.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu,  4 Jun 2026 07:15:07 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> Add the contended_release trace event. This tracepoint fires on the
> holder side when a contended lock is released, complementing the
> existing contention_begin/contention_end tracepoints which fire on the
> waiter side.
> 
> This enables correlating lock hold time under contention with waiter
> events by lock address.
> 
> Add trace_contended_release()/trace_call__contended_release() calls to
> the slowpath unlock paths of sleepable locks: mutex, rtmutex, semaphore,
> rwsem, percpu-rwsem, and RT-specific rwbase locks.
> 
> Where possible, trace_contended_release() fires before the lock is
> released and before the waiter is woken. For some lock types, the
> tracepoint fires after the release but before the wake. Making the
> placement consistent across all lock types is not worth the added
> complexity.
> 
> For reader/writer locks, the tracepoint fires for every reader releasing
> while a writer is waiting, not only for the last reader.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
> Acked-by: Paul E. McKenney <paulmck@kernel.org>

Acked-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM)
Re: [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM)
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new flag, __GFP_PRIVATE, which allows allocations to only access private memory nodes if the flag is provided. If not, it falls back to other nodes. This change also affects how pagecache pages are associated with VMAs and how file-backed allocation works in relation to mempolicies.

review_comments.author: Balbir Singh
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Balbir Singh noted that __GFP_PRIVATE might be too broad, as it doesn't account for the possibility of heterogeneous memory nodes, similar to how not all ZONE_DEVICE memory is homogeneous.

Reviewer Balbir Singh agrees with the current design but suggests that __GFP_PRIVATE should be an evolution of the current design, implying a need for further development and refinement.

Reviewer Balbir Singh split the discussion into two parts: unmapped page cache should not be impacted by mempolicy and shared pages' mempolicy would need to be on a set of nodes backed by private memory, depending on mbind() policy

Reviewer Balbir Singh expressed concern that unmapped page cache pages should not be allocated from or migrated to private memory nodes, requiring further consideration of this aspect.

Reviewer Balbir Singh expressed openness to the patch, but initially approached it from a different angle by suggesting mimicking N_MEMORY with N_MEMORY_PRIVATE and selectively modifying features.

Reviewer noted that file-backed pages are global resources and can be mapped into multiple VMAs, which could lead to incorrect node assignment if a process maps a file-backed page into private memory on one node and another process maps the same page into private memory on a different node.
review_comments.raw_body:
My concern is that __GFP_PRIVATE is too wide, I wonder if we'll have a
need to support N_MEMORY_PRIVATE may not be all homogeneous memory nodes.
Very similar to how not all ZONE_DEVICE memory is homogenous.

---

Agreed that we might get the design wrong, until we fix it up. I feel
that __GFP_PRIVATE should be an evolution of the design to that point.

---

Let's split this into two:

1. unmapped page cache is never impacted by mempolicy and should not
   end up on private memory nodes
2. For shared pages, mempolicy would be hard, but it would need to
   be on a set of nodes backed by private memory, depending on mbind()
   policy

---

I'd need to think more about this. For now, my basic requirement would
be that unmapped page cache should not come from/to private nodes.

---

I am open to this, I was coming from the blueprint approach of:
- Let's mimic N_MEMORY with N_MEMORY_PRIVATE and then pick and choose
  what features to change or make specific to the implementation

---

Thanks, I look forward to the next set of patches. Let me know if I
can help test what's on the list or if you want me to wait for the next
round

Balbir

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author is asking for clarification on reviewer's definition of 'homogeneous' in the context of private memory nodes, indicating a need to understand the feedback before responding.

Author acknowledges that if they cannot guarantee isolation without __GFP_PRIVATE, the baseline patch will not be merged and suggests that this flag is necessary for private memory nodes.

The author addressed Balbir Singh's concern about the interaction between file-backed VMAs and private memory nodes, explaining that silent migration can occur when a file is cached as unmapped page cache and then mapped at a later time. The author acknowledged that this issue needs to be considered in the design of mbind() support for private memory nodes.

The author addressed Balbir's concern that the private memory node design should mimic N_MEMORY, which allows normal memory handling. The author disagreed and explained that their design is 'closed by default', whereas N_MEMORY is 'open by default', to make reasoning about these nodes feasible.

The author is addressing concerns about the abstraction of memory allocation for various types of accelerators and special memory, such as network accelerators, GPUs, slow distributed memory, and compressed memory. The author explains that in most cases, a NUMA node is the right abstraction to reason about where memory should come from, allowing devices to be given private nodes to manage their own memory without worrying about general kernel operations.

Author acknowledges that the patch is complex and needs to be broken down into smaller features, such as reclaim and mempolicy, before further review can proceed.
review_comments.raw_body:
Can you more precise about your definition of homogeneous here?

Are you saying not all memory on a private node will be homogeneous?
   While possible, I would argue that you should not do this and
   should instead prefer to use multiple nodes - 1 per memory class.

Are you saying not all private nodes will be homogenous?
   I don't see the issue with this.

---

Possibly.  If we can't guarantee isolation without __GFP_PRIVATE, then
we probably can't merge the baseline without it.

---

This does not fully describe the problem.

A file can be opened and cached as unmapped page cache, and then mapped
at a later time - at which point the mapped copy would share the filemap
page cache page.

Worse, because it's file-backed, you can have the memory faulted onto
your remote node - reclaimed - and the faulted back in via the process
accessing the file via unmapped operations (read/write), at which point
you've had a silent migration occur.

Basically consider

Process A:
   fd = open("myfile", ..., RO);
   read(fd, ...);  /* mm/filemap.c fills page cache */

Process B:
   fd = open("myfile", ...);
   mem = mmap(fd, ...);
   mbind(mem, ..., private_node);
   for page in mem:
       int tmp = mem[page]; /* fault into vma */

The result of Process A running first is Process B thinks it has faulted
the memory onto private_node, but in reality it's taking soft faults and
just getting the filemap folio mapped in.

If you wanted mbind() support from the start, we would have to limit
applicability to anon memory only.

Shared anon memory is different, as there is a radix tree that deals
with a shared mempolicy state.

---

N_MEMORY essentially states:
	"This is normal memory touch it however you like"

N_MEMORY_PRIVATE (_MANAGED, w/e) says
	"This is NOT normal memory, there are special rules here"

So, no, lets not mimic N_MEMORY.  This is a "closed by default" design,
while N_MEMORY is an "open by default" design.  This design choice is
explicit to make reasoning about these nodes feasible.

---

So far i have collected:

- Network accelerators carrying their own memory for message buffers
- GPUs with semi-general-purpose working memory across coherent links
- Acceptionally slow distributed memory that you do not want fallback
  allocations to (so you want to deliberately tier what lands there)
- Compressed memory (just another form of accelerator really) which
  has *special access rules* (i.e. writes need to be controlled)

In most if not all of these cases, the right abstraction to reason about
where memory *should come from* IS a NUMA node.

- the network stack can be taught to check if the target device has a
  node with memory and prefer that node over local memory

- accelerators can be given private nodes to manage memory using
  core mm/ components, without worrying that general kernel operation
  will put unrelated memory on those nodes or do things like migrate
  your pages out from under you (unless your driver/service requested
  that).

the tiering application should be somewhat obvious / trivial.

---

Really I want to get the minimized set out the door so we can start
breaking this up by feature (reclaim, mempolicy, etc), because trying to
reason about it as a whole is infeasible - and I cannot be the single
arbiter of every use case (I simply do not have sufficient context).

I'm reworking it all as we speak.

~Gregory
